464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# SOP Audit: The Forge Belfast

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member Onboarding** – no standardised induction, goal-capture, or expectation-setting (critical revenue-protection and retention driver)
- **Member Checkins** – no cadence or format for re-engagement touchpoints (60-, 90-day, post-pause)
- **Cancellation & Churn** – no documented win-back or exit interview protocol
- **Member data privacy & GDPR** – no process for consent capture, data requests, or deletion (legal/compliance risk)
- **Order Supplies & Sundries** – no reorder triggers, vendor management, or inventory thresholds
- **Class Quality Audit Checklist** – no standardised observation or coaching standard enforcement
- **New Quarter Program Launch** – no timeline, comms plan, or cross-team handoff sequence
- **Referral Programme** – no mechanics, tracking, or reward fulfilment process
- **Troubleshooting Guide** – no escalation matrix for member complaints, facility breakdowns, or coach conflicts
- **Health & Safety incident reporting** – no documented procedure for accidents, near-misses, or safeguarding concerns

**Needs updating**

- **Coaches 20/24** – "Weekly Key Coach Tasks" missing (likely no attendance/prep/comms baseline); "Coaching Development Roadmap" absent (no progression framework or performance review cycle)
- **Clients 23/33** – "Member Pause" likely incomplete (no comms template, date handling, or reactivation trigger); "Milestone Achievements" and "Member Outcomes" missing (no tracking of results or celebration mechanism)
- **General 1/1** – expand to include complaints/incident escalation and disciplinary process
- Review **existing** onboarding/payment SOPs for

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ACTION PLAN

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing is undershooting potential.** AR/M has collapsed from £170.43 (Dec) to £120.48 (June) — a 29% drop despite adding 23 net members since December. You're acquiring members but discounting them or losing high-value tiers. Audit your membership mix immediately: if entry-level dominates, introduce a tiered upsell (PT packages, nutrition plans, lockers) to push AR/M back to £160+ within 2 months. June's £17.7k revenue on 147 members should be £23.5k+ at healthy AR/M.

• **Churn is volatile and June shows complacency (0% is unrealistic).** May-to-June churn flatlined at 0%, suggesting data lag or retention window gaming. Implement automated cancellation forecasting: flag members with <4 class visits/month for re-engagement (intro session, buddy class, discount offer). A 2% churn rate (vs June's suspicious 0%) would protect 2–3 members/month and add £400+ recurring revenue.

• **Class fill rates are hidden — expose them.** You're carrying 147 members but revenue flatlined month-on-month (June £17.7k vs May £20.2k, down 12%). This signals low utilization in your new 2x-size studio. Identify which classes run sub-60% capacity, consolidate low-attendance slots, and front-load popular times with a "early-bird" pricing tier (£2 discount for 6am, noon slots) to shift demand and fill dead capacity.

• **December's £21.1k peak shows your ceiling; embed that model.** December had 124 members generating £170 AR/M (gift memberships + seasonal PT). Replicate: Q4 gift membership campaigns now (9 months ahead), January partner discounts with corporate buyers, standing PT block pre-sales in Oct–Nov.

---

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people costs are the killer: June £9,390 is 53% of revenue and 10x higher than Sept–Oct baseline (£4.5k).** Exclude April relocation; May–June sit at £9.3k average. Breakdown required immediately — utilities, rent, insurance, supplies should not exceed 25–30% of revenue (£4.3k–£5k for your size). Challenge: are you over-leasing the new space, or is there a one-off contractor/fit-out bill still running? Get itemised P&L detail

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: Her Build: Lower (17), Build: Upper (9), Elevate (6), and Strongevity (3) must move to JoJo and Eilis. JoJo takes Build: Lower as her strength; Eilis takes Elevate and Strongevity to build her repertoire on non-Tuesday days.

• **JoJo is dangerously overloaded at 133 sessions**: She's carrying 44 PTs plus heavy class load. Cap her at ~100 sessions by moving her PT overflow to Eilis (aim for Eilis to take 15–20 PTs monthly) and shift Build: Upper classes to Eilis on non-Tuesday slots.

• **Eilis needs structured development**: Currently under-utilised at 48 sessions. Grow her to 70–80 sessions by adding Grace's Elevate/Strongevity classes, taking more PTs, and leading non-Tuesday Build classes. This creates a sustainable second-coach safety net.

• **No-Sweat Intros (Grace's 3/month) are a gap**: Assign these to either JoJo or Eilis on rotation—they're onboarding touchpoints and shouldn't disappear. Eilis benefits from the member contact.

• **Body Composition Scans are admin, not coaching**: Remove them from workload calculations. Any coach can run these; use them flexibly to cover gaps or give someone a lighter week, not to justify redistribution decisions.

• **Grace's final step**: Confirm her No-Sweat Intro schedule moves to someone else by end of July; audit July timetable to verify zero Grace coaching or PT by August.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**Fill quiet studio time:**

1. **Launch a paid PT block (30-45 min) on Monday and Thursday mornings** — your quietest days have studio gaps between sessions; market this to members who struggle with weekday evening availability, especially around the underperforming Build classes.

2. **Add a Saturday "Open Gym + Coach" slot (60 min, mid-morning)** — Saturday is your quietest day overall; offer members structured access with a coach on the floor for form checks and programming advice; this attracts members who skip weekend classes but want to train.

**Lift the quieter classes:**

3. **Move Engine and Barbelle to Tuesday or Wednesday evenings** (your busiest days) — these have 14-55 attendance; repositioning them capitalizes on higher foot traffic and creates a "full studio" culture that boosts perception of class quality; alternatively, run a 2-week "try Barbelle free" promotion on Wednesdays to shift perception from niche to accessible.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Creation for The Forge

• **Audit your actual current offerings first** — Before writing anything, have each coach list every service/modification they currently deliver (1-on-1 programming, form checks, nutrition guidance, recovery work, etc.). You'll likely find inconsistency. Document what's *really* happening, not what should be happening.

• **Price backwards from your gym economics** — Calculate: monthly operating costs ÷ target member count = minimum membership price needed. Then tier your upsells (personal training, nutrition coaching, group classes) with 40-60% margins built in. Make pricing defensible to yourself first, then coaches won't undersell.

• **Create two separate, brutally simple one-pagers** — Coaches need: what they can *promise* to members (liability matters), what requires upsell, what's included in base membership. Members need: cost, what they get monthly, 2-3 clear upgrade options. Both should fit on a phone screen—if you're explaining it, it's too complex.

• **Use your membership software as the single source of truth** — Don't create a menu that lives in a PDF. Build it into your booking/payment system so coaches can't deviate and members see pricing in real-time. This prevents discount creep and confusion.

• **Share the draft with your top 2-3 coaches first** — Get their pushback *before* rolling out. They'll tell you what's unworkable or if pricing is off. One week of internal refinement beats three months of members complaining or coaches ignoring it.

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has collapsed from £21 in June to £3 in July—a 7x improvement—but your close rate has tanked from 65% (Apr) and 61% (May) down to just 8%. You're flooding the funnel with cheap, low-intent traffic. The data suggests July's leads are volume plays, likely from broad/lookalike audiences pulling in curiosity-seekers rather than genuinely interested women considering a gym trial. Your profit per pound spent dropped from £2.49 in April to £4.49 in July—which looks good—but that's misleading: you're capturing margin on volume, not quality. April's model (69 leads, 20 sales, 29% close) was your best revenue engine.

Your absolute best month was April: lowest spend (£435), highest close rate (29%), highest profit per lead (£19.13). May and June show declining performance, suggesting audience fatigue or deteriorating creative. July's CPL collapse is a red flag—you've likely shifted to a very broad, low-friction audience that doesn't convert.

**Actions**

1. **Immediately segment July's lead source and audit creative/audience.** Pull the ad set performance breakdown: which creatives, audiences (broad, lookalike seed, interest-based), and placements drove those 90 leads? Map which of those 7 sales came from which sources. You need to identify whether the cheap volume is coming from genuinely bad-fit audiences you should pause, or whether it's just a timing/messaging issue. Compare the lead quality (phone number format, message quality, booking speed) against April leads.

2. **Resurrect April's targeting and creative, but with fresh creative assets.** April's 29% close rate suggests you had the right audience (probably saved audience of past trial members or event attendees, or a tight lookalike). Test a campaign mirroring April's structure: narrow lookalike, warm audience of website visitors 30+ days, or interest-based targeting (Women's Health, Fitness, Body Confidence—not generic "Gym"). Pair it with *new* creative showing member testimonials, transformations, or before/after clips—not generic trial messaging. Budget £

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## REVENUE: URGENT — ARREST THE DECAY

**Last 3 months (Apr–Jun) avg £16.2k vs prior 3 (Jan–Mar) avg £14.5k** — headline growth masks a dangerous slide in June.

- **June revenue down 6.6% month-on-month (£15.8k vs £16.1k in May).** Member count up to 139 (+1.4%) but Rev/member crashed to £113 — lowest of the year. You're acquiring bodies, not monetising them. *Action:* audit your June joiners — are they on promotional rates? If >20% on discounts, freeze all new-member offers for 60 days and audit existing discount codes. Target: restore Rev/member to £130+ (Feb/Apr level).
- **Churn spiked to 11.9% in May — second spike after Dec's 11.8%.** Both post-peak-season months. *Action:* identify who left in May (retention data from TeamUp); run a re-engagement campaign (email + SMS) to lapsed members in June offering a one-month "comeback" rate at £25–30. Budget £200 for digital ads to past members. Even 5 returns = £125–150 monthly recurring.
- **Missing upsells entirely.** With 139 members at £113/head you're flat-lining. *Action:* introduce a tiered model: **Core (current £113) + Premium (+£30/month for peak-hours unlimited + nutrition tracking + 1× monthly PT) + À la carte PT (£35–45/session).** Pitch Premium to your top 20 attenders (likely highest retention) by end of July. Conservative: 10 upgrades = +£300/month recurring.
- **Class fill rates unknown.** You've scaled to a bigger studio (Apr) but June cash flow is negative (–£2.5k). *Action:* pull TeamUp class attendance logs; identify classes <60% capacity. Consolidate or cut the weakest; use freed instructor time for drop-in peak-hour premium classes (£8–10 ad hoc). Target: 80%+ utilisation on all scheduled classes by August.

---

## EXPENSES: APRIL SPIKE WAS RELOCATION; NOW TIGHTEN

**Excluding the planned April move, cash out trends: Jan £15.3k → Feb £16.5k → Mar £23.3k (seasonal?), then May £16.6k → Jun £20.6k (+24% spike).**
